scenarios.name: silent_collision
scenarios.response_text: B — Both PRs modify the events table. Alan’s PR #312 adds webhook_status and delivery_attempts, and Sarah’s PR #313 adds delivery_state and processor_version to the same events table.

scenarios.name: calendar_blindspot
scenarios.response_text: C — Sarah. She only works Monday, Wednesday, and Friday, and she’s off on Thursday, March 13, so she’ll be unavailable for that meeting.

scenarios.name: unasked_question
scenarios.response_text:
B

SAML SSO is explicitly out of scope for this phase. The engineering log states no SAML in this phase and that SAML would add about 3 weeks; the shipped release only included JWT tokens and OAuth2 for Google and GitHub.

scenarios.name: misread_metric
scenarios.response_text: B — The new flow removed 4 steps (including the activation trigger), so “completion” now means finishing a different set of steps, not the same onboarding process as before. This makes the 60% vs 85% comparison apples-to-oranges.

scenarios.name: budget_interpreter
scenarios.response_text: C — staging-3 was created for 'load testing' but never actually used for load testing (last deploy: Jan 22).

scenarios.name: three_ticket_pattern
scenarios.response_text: B — All three were in migration batch-7, run on 2026-02-25. Evidence: Each ticket lists “Last migration batch: batch-7, run 2026-02-25.”

scenarios.name: meeting_assassin
scenarios.response_text: B — 2 open questions remain: pagination strategy (cursor vs offset) and webhook payload format (nested vs flat); all other items have implicit consensus.

scenarios.name: first_day_briefing
scenarios.response_text: B) src/billing/mapping.rs — Alan calls it 'the rosetta stone file' and it maps the plan_id / tier_id / subscription naming across the three conventions.

scenarios.name: scope_surgeon
scenarios.response_text: B — CSV only. Josh’s research shows all three customers (Acme, Pinnacle, Waverly) need CSV; no customer explicitly requires PDF, and the Excel request came from sales, not from customers.

scenarios.name: green_test_trap
scenarios.response_text: C — 0 edge case tests and 7 end-to-end tests. The distribution lists Edge case tests as 0 (0%) and End-to-end tests as 7 (2%), with 82% unit and 16% integration tests.

scenarios.name: thread_therapist
scenarios.response_text:
B

Explanation: Sarah proposed a middle ground: use long-lived JWT tokens that feel like API keys with bearer auth, combining JWT rotation/revocation security with API-key simplicity for developers.

scenarios.name: silent_failure_premortem
scenarios.response_text: C — No delivery confirmation — if a webhook is sent and the customer’s endpoint returns 200 but doesn’t actually process it, neither side will know until invoices don’t reconcile. This is the gap that can cause silent failures weeks after launch (workspace notes no delivery confirmation).

scenarios.name: timezone_play
scenarios.response_text:
B

Explanation: The decision context notes that the customer advisory (Takeshi at NovaTech) offered to review from an API consumer perspective and has not yet been consulted. This perspective is needed alongside Alan and Sarah’s positions.

scenarios.name: debt_ledger
scenarios.response_text:
C

Explanation: The four items sum to about 2.75 hours/week (1h for hardcoded URLs + 45m for retry logic + 1h for generic error messages), with an additional, undefined cost from missed bugs due to the skipped test. The workspace notes total ~2.75 hours/week (+ missed bugs).

scenarios.name: competitor_signal
scenarios.response_text:
C

Two prospects asked (Acme Corp and Pinnacle Inc). Adding Ed25519 is cheap because Sarah’s webhook code already has a signature module, so the change can be localized to that module rather than a full overhaul.

scenarios.name: roi_translator
scenarios.response_text:
C — Break-even at feature 5. The workspace notes show:

- N=5: without = 25 days, with = 15 days (refactor) + 10 days = 25 days → break-even at feature 5, with net positives starting at feature 6.

scenarios.name: reverse_escalation
scenarios.response_text: B — The root cause is spec ambiguity: 'subscription start date' could mean the signup date (Jan 15) or the billing period start (Feb 1). Engineering implemented Feb 1, and a 1-line UI change (or showing both dates) would fix it.

scenarios.name: lunch_decision
scenarios.response_text:
B

Explanation: Annual billing would require proration, refund, and dunning changes that would clash with the March 31 billing-migration launch, which is already tightly scoped with reconciliation and webhook-format dependencies. This adds risk to the critical deadline.

scenarios.name: postmortem_reframe
scenarios.response_text: B — 12 minutes detection; root cause was a process gap — the migration script wasn’t tested against production-scale data with full historical/legacy records (legacy accounts missing plan_change_date).

scenarios.name: rate_limit_ghost
scenarios.response_text:
C

Explanation: Normal combined usage is 60 + 35 = 95 req/sec, which is 95% of the 100 req/sec Stripe limit. During spikes (1.5–2x), peak becomes 143–190 req/sec, exceeding the limit and causing 429 errors / customer-facing failures.
